skill.name: file-organization
skill.description: Organize project folders, file names, and conventions for maintainable frontend and backend codebases.
skill.summary: Plan and standardize project file structures for growing software teams.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review shows the cited lines are Markdown examples, file tree templates, reference URLs, and naming guidance rather than executable code. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious network behavior was found. The remaining risk is legitimate but elevated because the skill declares Read, Write, Glob, and Bash access for project reorganization tasks.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem and Bash Access
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Read, Write, Glob, and Bash access while advising project structure changes. This is appropriate for file organization, but it can move or rewrite project files if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool declaration is explicit and the stated use cases involve restructuring project files. I found no malicious instructions, so the concern is operational risk rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Flags Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick execution alerts correspond to Markdown code fences and inline formatting in documentation examples. They do not instruct execution of untrusted commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are visible Markdown structure diagrams, naming examples, and tags. No executable script, dynamic shell construction, or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Network Flags Are Reference Links and Example Constants
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are a placeholder API constant and public documentation references. I found no request logic, webhook target, or instruction to send project data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in a naming convention snippet and a references section. There is no surrounding code that performs network access or transmits data.

low_findings.title: Sensitive and Path Traversal Flags Are Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The environment file, browser storage, path traversal, and weak cryptography alerts map to example file names or ordinary words in project templates. No secret reading, traversal routine, or cryptographic algorithm is implemented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are template names such as useLocalStorage, crypto.ts, .env.example, and a relative import example. Review found no code that opens secrets or normalizes attacker-controlled paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Tooling Enables Project-Wide File Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can be paired with write-capable tools to reorganize folders and files. Users should review planned moves before applying changes to avoid accidental data loss.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission line and project template show expected filesystem work. The risk depends on user authorization and execution context, not malicious code in the skill.

content.value_statement: Growing projects become hard to navigate when files and folders use mixed patterns. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code propose clear structures, naming rules, and refactoring plans.
actual_capabilities: Suggests React and Next.js folder structures for app, component, lib, type, and config areas.
Outlines Node.js and Express backend folders for routes, controllers, services, repositories, and tests.
Recommends feature-based structures for larger applications with shared and core modules.
Defines practical file and folder naming conventions for teams.
Explains when to use barrel files and when broad re-exports can harm tree shaking.
Provides path alias guidance for cleaner imports.
limitations: Does not inspect architecture automatically unless the user provides repository context.
Does not guarantee framework-specific best practices beyond the examples in the skill.
Can suggest file moves, but users must approve destructive or large changes.
Does not include migration scripts or dependency graph analysis.

use_cases.title: Start a New Product Codebase
use_cases.description: Create a clear initial folder layout before the first sprint starts.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Growing Application
use_cases.description: Move from broad folders to feature-based modules as product complexity increases.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Conventions
use_cases.description: Document consistent naming, nesting, and import rules for shared development workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the file-organization skill to propose a simple folder structure for my new web application. Include naming rules and a short reason for each main folder.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Current Layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the file-organization skill to review this project tree. Identify confusing folders, excessive nesting, and naming inconsistencies. Suggest a safer target structure.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Feature-Based Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the file-organization skill to convert this application into a feature-based layout. List the proposed feature folders, shared areas, and migration order.

prompt_templates.title: Define Team Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the file-organization skill to create file, folder, import alias, and barrel export conventions for a team working across frontend and backend services.

output_examples.input: A small Next.js app needs a maintainable starting layout.
output_examples.output: A concise project structure with separate app routes, reusable UI components, shared libraries, typed models, configuration files, and global styles.

output_examples.input: A backend service has routes, services, and database files mixed together.
output_examples.output: A migration plan that separates API routes, controllers, business services, repositories, models, configuration, utilities, and tests.

output_examples.input: A team wants consistent naming rules across projects.
output_examples.output: A convention guide covering component names, hooks, utility files, constants, type files, test files, folder casing, and import aliases.

best_practices: Ask for the current project tree before recommending a refactor.
Review planned moves before writing or deleting files.
Keep folder depth limited and place related files near the feature they support.
anti_patterns: Do not reorganize a repository without checking imports, tests, and build configuration.
Do not create generic folders such as misc or helpers when a specific name is available.
Do not use barrel exports everywhere when they hide dependencies or harm tree shaking.

faq.question: Can this skill move files automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide agents that have write access, but users should approve every move plan first.

faq.question: Is this only for React projects?
faq.answer: No. It includes React and Next.js examples, plus Node.js, Express, and feature-based structures.

faq.question: Does it replace architecture review?
faq.answer: No. It helps with organization patterns, but architecture decisions still need project-specific review.

faq.question: Can it help with naming conventions?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers file names, folder names, component names, hooks, constants, and test files.

faq.question: Will it update imports after files move?
faq.answer: The skill can suggest import alias patterns, but import updates require separate repository-aware work.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it requests broad filesystem and Bash tools for project reorganization.
